sessions.title: Leadership Awareness Check
sessions.principle:
Leadership begins with ownership, not status.
Leadership is not a title you wear. It is responsibility you carry.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leadership starts with responsibility.
Leadership begins before recognition.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Answer honestly: What makes someone a leader? What behaviors destroy leadership? Which leadership habits do you show right now?
questions: What habits build leadership?
What habits weaken trust?
What responsibilities matter most?
Which leadership habits do you actually show right now?
What must change first?

scripture.text: Whoever wants to become great among you must be your servant.

sessions.title: Leading Yourself First
sessions.principle:
Athletes who cannot lead themselves cannot lead others.
Self-leadership means controlling effort, attitude, and discipline without needing constant correction.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we lead ourselves first.
Self-control is the root of leadership.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What does self-leadership look like in daily training?
questions: What habits show self-leadership?
What habits weaken it?
Where do you need the most work?
What does self-leadership look like when nobody is watching?

scripture.text: Whoever rules his spirit is better than he who takes a city.

sessions.title: Leadership Through Example
sessions.principle:
People follow what they consistently see.
Athletes trust leaders whose work, discipline, and respect show up every day.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, example leads first.
Example carries more weight than instruction.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What behaviors make someone worth following?
questions: What behaviors earn trust?
What behaviors weaken influence?
What do others consistently see from you?
What example do you set when no one is praising you?

scripture.text: Set an example for the believers.

sessions.title: Responsibility Without Recognition
sessions.principle:
Real leadership works without needing applause.
Leaders carry weight even when nobody notices.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we carry responsibility quietly.
The strongest leadership often looks quiet.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How do leaders act when nobody is praising them?
questions: Why do some athletes need praise to lead?
What makes quiet leadership strong?
What responsibility do you avoid when no one is watching?
What habit would prove quiet leadership in you?

scripture.text: Your Father sees what is done in secret.

sessions.title: Trust and Reliability
sessions.principle:
Reliable athletes become trusted leaders.
Teammates follow athletes they can count on, not athletes who are strong only when convenient.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, reliability earns influence.
Reliability is leadership people can feel.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What habits make an athlete reliable enough to lead?
questions: What builds reliability?
What breaks trust?
What reliability habit do you need most?
How does dependable behavior create influence?

scripture.text: A faithful person will be richly blessed.

sessions.title: Service Over Status
sessions.principle:
Leadership is service before it is influence.
Leaders help the room improve. They do not use the room to feed ego.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders serve first.
Service makes leadership credible.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What does serving the team actually look like in practice?
questions: What does service look like?
What looks like status-chasing instead?
What one act of service matters most in your room?
How does service build real influence?

scripture.text: Serve one another humbly in love.

sessions.title: Emotional Control in Leaders
sessions.principle:
Undisciplined emotion weakens leadership.
Athletes copy the emotional habits of the people they trust.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders stay composed.
Composure protects influence.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Why is emotional control essential for leadership?
questions: What emotional habits weaken leadership?
What emotional habits strengthen it?
What do others learn from your reactions?
Where do you most need better composure?

scripture.text: A person without self-control is like a city broken into.

sessions.title: Responsibility at a Higher Standard
sessions.principle:
Leadership raises the standard, not lowers it.
The more influence an athlete has, the more responsibility they carry for culture, effort, and trust.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leadership means higher responsibility.
Influence multiplies responsibility.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What responsibilities increase when someone becomes a leader in the room?
questions: What responsibilities grow with leadership?
Why do some athletes resist them?
Which one matters most to you?
What standard rises first when influence increases?

scripture.text: To whom much is given, much will be required.

sessions.title: Leading Partners Safely
sessions.principle:
Leadership shows up in how you handle the person across from you.
Leaders protect training partners, give honest work, and set the tone for safe, disciplined practice.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders protect the rep.
Leadership is visible in the rep before it is visible in the room.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How should leaders train with partners differently from immature athletes?
questions: What makes partner leadership visible?
What destroys partner trust?
What habit must you improve?
How does safe leadership shape the tone of the room?

scripture.text: Do to others as you would have them do to you.

sessions.title: Helping Without Arrogance
sessions.principle:
Leaders help others without acting superior.
Correction without humility becomes ego.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders help without arrogance.
Humility makes leadership teachable.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How can a leader help a partner improve without sounding arrogant?
questions: What makes help feel disrespectful?
What makes it useful?
How should maturity sound?
What tone helps without humiliating?

scripture.text: Do nothing out of selfish ambition or vain conceit.

sessions.title: Protecting Beginners
sessions.principle:
Leadership protects the newest people in the room.
Beginners often learn culture from the first athletes they touch.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders protect beginners.
How you treat beginners reveals your stewardship.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How should a leader train with a newer athlete?
questions: What damages a beginner’s trust?
What strengthens it?
What should a leader never do?
How should beginners experience your leadership?

scripture.text: Whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me.

sessions.title: Raising the Partner Standard
sessions.principle:
Leaders bring the level up without turning everything into ego.
Strong athletes raise the quality of the rep through honest effort, clear tone, and disciplined pace.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders raise the level.
Good leaders raise the rep without hijacking it.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How can a leader raise a partner’s level during training?
questions: What raises the rep level?
What ruins it?
What leadership habit matters most here?
How do you challenge without turning the rep into ego?

scripture.text: Encourage one another and build each other up.

sessions.title: Tone of Voice and Leadership
sessions.principle:
Leadership is heard in tone as much as content.
Tone can build trust or poison it.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders speak with discipline.
Tone reveals maturity before words do.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How does tone of voice affect leadership in training?
questions: What tone builds trust?
What tone damages it?
What tone do you default to?
How should mature leadership sound?

scripture.text: Let your conversation be always full of grace.

sessions.title: Leading in Hard Rounds
sessions.principle:
Leadership matters more when rounds get hard.
Leaders are revealed when pace, fatigue, and frustration rise.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leadership stays steady under pressure.
Pressure reveals the truth of leadership.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What does leadership look like during hard, emotional, or exhausting rounds?
questions: What usually breaks first?
What should stay fixed?
What do others learn from your pressure habits?
What does your behavior teach in hard rounds?

scripture.text: Consider it pure joy whenever you face trials.

sessions.title: Listening as Leadership
sessions.principle:
Leaders who cannot listen eventually stop growing.
Leadership is not only giving direction. It is also receiving correction.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders stay teachable.
Teachable leaders stay usable.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Why is listening an important leadership habit?
questions: Why do some leaders stop listening?
What damage follows?
What does teachable leadership look like?
How does listening protect growth?

scripture.text: The wise listen to advice.

sessions.title: Partner Trust Through Leadership
sessions.principle:
Partners should feel safer, sharper, and more focused with a real leader.
Leadership in partner work is measured by the trust people feel when training with you.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leadership earns partner trust.
Trust is leadership people can feel in real time.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What habits make a leader trustworthy in partner work?
questions: What habits create partner trust?
What habits damage it quickly?
What one trust habit matters most for you?
How should trust feel to someone training with you?

scripture.text: Whoever walks in integrity walks securely.

sessions.title: Raising the Team Standard
sessions.principle:
Leaders raise the room by raising the standard of daily behavior.
Teams rise when leaders work at a level others can feel and follow.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders raise the standard.
The room rises to what leadership repeatedly tolerates or models.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How does a leader raise the standard of the room without needing a speech?
questions: What behaviors lift the standard?
What behaviors quietly lower it?
What standard do you currently reinforce?
What do others feel from your daily example?

scripture.text: Set an example.

sessions.title: Being Reliable for the Team
sessions.principle:
Teams trust leaders who are dependable in effort and behavior.
Reliability gives your influence weight.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders are dependable.
Dependability turns influence into trust.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What habits make a leader dependable for the team?
questions: What builds dependability?
What breaks it?
Where are you inconsistent?
What would make teammates trust you more?

scripture.text: Many claim to have unfailing love, but a faithful person who can find?

sessions.title: Serving Team Morale
sessions.principle:
Leaders affect team morale through daily attitude.
Strong leaders stabilize morale without feeding false hype.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders protect morale with truth and effort.
Real morale grows from truth plus effort.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How can leaders strengthen team morale without becoming fake or soft?
questions: What strengthens morale?
What weakens it?
What does mature encouragement sound like?
How can truth and effort work together?

scripture.text: Encourage one another daily.

sessions.title: Shared Responsibility in Leadership
sessions.principle:
Leadership is not doing everything alone; it is helping others carry responsibility well.
Leaders strengthen the team by increasing ownership in others.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders multiply responsibility.
The best leaders make the room stronger, not more dependent.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How should a leader help teammates carry more responsibility?
questions: What makes teams too dependent on one person?
What creates shared ownership?
How can you multiply responsibility?
What can you stop doing that creates dependence?

scripture.text: Equip his people for works of service.

sessions.title: Correcting Teammates with Respect
sessions.principle:
Leaders correct behavior without humiliating people.
Strong leaders protect standards and dignity at the same time.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders correct with respect.
Correction without respect weakens leadership.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How should a leader correct a teammate when standards slip?
questions: What makes correction destructive?
What makes it useful?
What does respectful accountability sound like?
How do you protect dignity while protecting standards?

scripture.text: Wounds from a friend can be trusted.

sessions.title: Protecting Team Culture
sessions.principle:
Leaders protect the culture that protects the team.
Culture weakens when standards go unguarded.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders protect culture.
Culture survives through disciplined protection.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What behaviors protect team culture and what behaviors quietly damage it?
questions: What strengthens culture?
What slowly damages it?
What must leaders guard most?
What drift have you seen that needs early response?

scripture.text: Stand firm in the faith.

sessions.title: Handling Resistance from Teammates
sessions.principle:
Leaders will face resistance and must stay disciplined through it.
Leadership requires steadiness when pushback comes.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders stay steady under resistance.
Leadership is tested most when others push back.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How should a leader respond when teammates resist correction or responsibility?
questions: Why do teammates resist leadership?
What response weakens leadership?
What response protects it?
How do you return to standards without ego?

scripture.text: A gentle answer turns away wrath.

sessions.title: Accountability Without Ego
sessions.principle:
Leaders must hold standards without turning correction into self-importance.
Mature accountability is about strengthening the person and the room.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, accountability serves the standard, not the ego.
Humility keeps accountability clean.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What is the difference between accountability with humility and accountability with ego?
questions: What makes accountability ego-driven?
What makes it mature?
Which side do you lean toward?
What motive usually drives your correction?

scripture.text: Do nothing out of selfish ambition.

sessions.title: When to Speak and When to Stay Quiet
sessions.principle:
Leadership requires timing, not just truth.
Wisdom protects both truth and relationship.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders use timing with discipline.
Truth lands better when timing is disciplined.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How can a leader know when to speak immediately and when to wait?
questions: What makes timing poor?
What makes it wise?
Why does timing matter in leadership?
What happens when truth is delivered at the wrong time?

scripture.text: There is a time to be silent and a time to speak.

sessions.title: Carrying Standards When Coaches Aren’t Watching
sessions.principle:
Real leaders carry standards without supervision.
Leadership becomes real when athletes protect discipline even when coaches are absent or distracted.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, standards stay alive without supervision.
Integrity-led leadership lives without an audience.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What should leaders protect when coaches are not right there to correct the room?
questions: What usually slips when supervision fades?
What should remain fixed?
What does integrity-led leadership look like?
What would the room lose if you drift when coaches are away?

scripture.text: Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart.

sessions.title: Protecting Teammates from Drift
sessions.principle:
Leaders notice when teammates start slipping before the damage becomes normal.
Leadership notices drift early.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders notice drift early.
Small drift becomes culture when no one challenges it.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What signs show that a teammate or a room is starting to drift?
questions: What signs of drift are easiest to ignore?
Why is early response important?
How should leaders respond?
What repeated small habits become dangerous if ignored?

scripture.text: Catch for us the foxes, the little foxes that ruin the vineyards.

sessions.title: Leading Different Personalities
sessions.principle:
Leaders must learn how to help different kinds of teammates.
Mature leadership adapts without lowering standards.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders adapt without compromising standards.
Adaptability makes leadership more useful, not softer.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Why does leadership require different approaches for different teammates?
questions: Why does one approach not fit everyone?
What should remain constant?
How can leaders adapt wisely?
What kind of teammate do you need to learn to lead better?

scripture.text: The wise in heart are called discerning.

sessions.title: Leadership Under Pressure
sessions.principle:
Pressure exposes the truth of a leader’s maturity.
Anyone can look like a leader when things are easy.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders stay steady under pressure.
Pressure reveals whether leadership is built or borrowed.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What should leadership look like when the room is tired, frustrated, or losing momentum?
questions: What breaks first under pressure?
What must stay fixed?
What do teammates need from leaders in hard moments?
What does pressure reveal about your current leadership?

scripture.text: When you are tested, your faith produces perseverance.

sessions.title: Stabilizing the Room
sessions.principle:
Strong leaders help stabilize rooms during chaos, conflict, or low morale.
Leaders who stabilize the room become anchors for culture.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders stabilize the room.
Steady leadership keeps chaos from becoming culture.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How can a leader stabilize the room during hard days or chaotic moments?
questions: What destabilizes a room?
What steadies it?
How can leadership become an anchor?
What do people feel from a stable leader?

scripture.text: God is not a God of disorder but of peace.

sessions.title: Protecting the Weakest Link
sessions.principle:
Leadership serves weaker teammates without feeding weakness.
Strong leaders protect and develop weaker athletes without mocking them or carrying them irresponsibly.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders protect and develop the weaker link.
Good leadership strengthens weakness without worshiping it.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How should a leader help weaker or struggling teammates without enabling laziness?
questions: What helps weaker teammates improve?
What enables weakness?
What does mature service look like?
How do you support effort without lowering standards?

scripture.text: Strengthen the weak hands.

sessions.title: When Leadership Costs You
sessions.principle:
Leadership often costs comfort, popularity, or convenience.
Real leadership sometimes means standing firm when it would be easier to stay quiet.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders carry cost with courage.
Leadership without cost is usually performance, not stewardship.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What kinds of cost come with real leadership, and why must leaders accept them?
questions: Why do some athletes avoid leadership cost?
What cost matters most in your room?
What does courage-led leadership look like?
What cost have you been avoiding?

scripture.text: Be strong and courageous.

sessions.title: Leadership Reputation
sessions.principle:
Leaders build reputations through repeated behavior.
Leadership reputation is earned rep by rep.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leadership reputation is earned by behavior.
Leadership reputation is written by repeated behavior.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What reputation do you think you have as a leader, and what reputation do you want to earn instead?
questions: What reputation do your habits build?
What do people likely trust you for?
What must change?
What kind of leader do others likely experience you as right now?

scripture.text: A good name is more desirable than great riches.

sessions.title: Leadership and Humility Together
sessions.principle:
Leadership and humility are not opposites; they need each other.
Without humility, leadership becomes ego. Without courage, humility becomes passivity.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders stay humble and strong.
Humility keeps leadership clean and usable.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Why must leadership and humility stay together?
questions: What happens when leadership loses humility?
What happens when humility loses courage?
What does healthy balance look like?
Where are you currently out of balance?

scripture.text: Clothe yourselves with humility.

sessions.title: Multiplying Leadership
sessions.principle:
Good leaders do not hoard influence; they develop more leaders.
The healthiest rooms are built around shared leadership and multiplied responsibility.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders multiply leadership.
The best leaders leave the room stronger, not more dependent.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How can a leader help grow leadership in others?
questions: What keeps teams too dependent on one leader?
What grows shared leadership?
What can you start doing now?
How can you invite responsibility in others?

scripture.text: Teach others also.

sessions.title: Leadership in Competition Environments
sessions.principle:
Leadership must survive outside normal practice comfort.
Competition, travel, nerves, and chaos reveal whether leadership carries into real environments.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders represent the team under pressure.
Real leadership travels well under pressure.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What should leadership look like in competition settings, not just in practice?
questions: What changes in competition environments?
What should remain the same?
How can leaders represent the room well?
What does pressure expose about leadership outside practice?

scripture.text: Conduct yourselves in a manner worthy.

sessions.title: What Teammates Need from Leaders
sessions.principle:
Leadership is measured by what teammates actually receive from you.
Strong leaders think about what the room actually needs.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders give the room what it truly needs.
Useful leadership is more valuable than impressive leadership.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What do teammates need most from leaders during normal training and hard moments?
questions: What does the room truly need?
What do immature leaders focus on instead?
What can you provide more consistently?
How do you become more useful to the room?

scripture.text: Look not only to your own interests.

sessions.title: Leadership Standard Statement
sessions.principle:
Leaders need a clear standard to live by.
A clear standard protects the room and the leader.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leaders live by a clear standard.
Clear standards create trustworthy leadership.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Write your leadership standard in one strong sentence.
questions: What should teammates always get from you?
What weak habits must disappear?
What standard will you live by?
Can someone else clearly see that standard?

scripture.text: Be steadfast in your work.

sessions.title: What Does Real Leadership Look Like?
sessions.principle:
Real leadership is disciplined, useful, humble, and trustworthy.
The strongest leaders are not the loudest. They are the ones whose habits strengthen the room consistently.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, real leadership strengthens the room.
Leadership is visible in repeated action.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Define real leadership in actions, not titles. List 5 behaviors you will live out next week.
questions: What does real leadership look like?
What behavior proves it?
What weak habits must disappear?
What would others clearly see if you lived this out?

scripture.text: By their fruit you will recognize them.

sessions.title: Leadership Retest
sessions.principle:
Leadership growth should be visible and measurable.
The goal is not image. The goal is responsibility, usefulness, and stewardship.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, leadership grows through responsibility and service.
Growth becomes visible through reflection.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Repeat Session 1 exactly. Answer again: What makes someone a leader? What behaviors destroy leadership? Which leadership habits do you show right now? Then compare your old answer to your new one.
questions: What changed in your leadership understanding?
What habits improved most?
What leadership responsibility still needs work?
What is your next-step leadership commitment?

scripture.text: Well done, good and faithful servant.
